title: National Taiwan University

infobox.value: [Urban](/wiki/Urban_area "Urban area"),  1.6 km2 (0.62 sq mi) ([Greater Taipei](/wiki/Taipei%E2%80%93Keelung_metropolitan_area "Taipei–Keelung metropolitan area") combined);  344 km2 (133 sq mi) ([Nantou County](/wiki/Nantou_County "Nantou County") combined)

* [APRU](/wiki/APRU "APRU") * [AEARU](/wiki/AEARU "AEARU") * [ASAIHL](/wiki/Association_of_Southeast_Asian_Institutions_of_Higher_Learning "Association of Southeast Asian Institutions of Higher Learning") * [AACSB](/wiki/Association_to_Advance_Collegiate_Schools_of_Business "Association to Advance Collegiate Schools of Business") * [EUTW](/wiki/EUTW "EUTW")

(**NTU**; [Chinese](/wiki/Traditional_Chinese_characters "Traditional Chinese characters"): 國立臺灣大學) is a [national](/wiki/National_university "National university") [public](/wiki/Public_university "Public university") [research university](/wiki/Research_university "Research university") with its main campus in [Taipei](/wiki/Taipei "Taipei"), Taiwan.[[8]](#cite_note-9) Founded in 1928 during [Japanese rule](/wiki/Taiwan_under_Japanese_rule "Taiwan under Japanese rule") as **Taihoku Imperial University** (臺北帝國大學), the seventh of the [Imperial Universities](/wiki/Imperial_Universities "Imperial Universities") of the [Empire of Japan](/wiki/Empire_of_Japan "Empire of Japan"), it is the oldest university in Taiwan and is supervised by the [Ministry of Education](/wiki/Ministry_of_Education_(Taiwan) "Ministry of Education (Taiwan)").

National university located in Taipei, Taiwan

"University of Taiwan" redirects here. For all universities and colleges in Taiwan, see [List of universities and colleges in Taiwan](/wiki/List_of_universities_and_colleges_in_Taiwan "List of universities and colleges in Taiwan").

Not to be confused with [National Taipei University](/wiki/National_Taipei_University "National Taipei University"), [Nanyang Technological University](/wiki/Nanyang_Technological_University "Nanyang Technological University"), or [National Taiwan Normal University](/wiki/National_Taiwan_Normal_University "National Taiwan Normal University").

**National Taiwan University** (**NTU**; [Chinese](/wiki/Traditional_Chinese_characters "Traditional Chinese characters"): 國立臺灣大學) is a [national](/wiki/National_university "National university") [public](/wiki/Public_university "Public university") [research university](/wiki/Research_university "Research university") with its main campus in [Taipei](/wiki/Taipei "Taipei"), Taiwan.[[8]](#cite_note-9) Founded in 1928 during [Japanese rule](/wiki/Taiwan_under_Japanese_rule "Taiwan under Japanese rule") as **Taihoku Imperial University** (臺北帝國大學), the seventh of the [Imperial Universities](/wiki/Imperial_Universities "Imperial Universities") of the [Empire of Japan](/wiki/Empire_of_Japan "Empire of Japan"), it is the oldest university in Taiwan and is supervised by the [Ministry of Education](/wiki/Ministry_of_Education_(Taiwan) "Ministry of Education (Taiwan)").

The university has three major campuses in Taipei and operates [satellite campuses](/wiki/Satellite_campus "Satellite campus") across the country. It hosts over 200 degree programs and consists of 17 colleges, including the [College of Medicine](/wiki/National_Taiwan_University_College_of_Medicine "National Taiwan University College of Medicine"), and 61 departments, 152 affiliated [research institutes](/wiki/Research_institute "Research institute"), and more than 100 other national research centers, such as the [National Taiwan University Hospital](/wiki/National_Taiwan_University_Hospital "National Taiwan University Hospital").[[9]](#cite_note-:2-10) Its [financial endowment](/wiki/Financial_endowment "Financial endowment") of NT$67.3 billion (US$2.24 billion) is the largest in the country and [one of the largest in Asia](/wiki/Lists_of_institutions_of_higher_education_by_endowment_size "Lists of institutions of higher education by endowment size").

National Taiwan University has institutional affiliations with the [Harvard–Yenching Institute](/wiki/Harvard%E2%80%93Yenching_Institute "Harvard–Yenching Institute"),[[10]](#cite_note-11) the [Max Planck Society](/wiki/Max_Planck_Society "Max Planck Society"), the [Institute for Advanced Study](/wiki/Institute_for_Advanced_Study "Institute for Advanced Study"), and produces the [Performance Ranking of Scientific Papers for World Universities](/wiki/Performance_Ranking_of_Scientific_Papers_for_World_Universities "Performance Ranking of Scientific Papers for World Universities").[[11]](#cite_note-12) It publishes academic works through its [university press](/wiki/University_press "University press"), the [National Taiwan University Press](/wiki/National_Taiwan_University_Press "National Taiwan University Press"). In 2015, NTU formed a [university system](/wiki/University_system "University system") with the [National Taiwan University of Science and Technology](/wiki/National_Taiwan_University_of_Science_and_Technology "National Taiwan University of Science and Technology") and [National Taiwan Normal University](/wiki/National_Taiwan_Normal_University "National Taiwan Normal University").

[Notable graduates of the university](/wiki/List_of_National_Taiwan_University_people "List of National Taiwan University people") include five [presidents of the Republic of China](/wiki/Presidents_of_the_Republic_of_China "Presidents of the Republic of China"), six [vice-presidents of the Republic of China](/wiki/Vice_President_of_the_Republic_of_China "Vice President of the Republic of China"), more than 120 members of [Academia Sinica](/wiki/Academia_Sinica "Academia Sinica"), and 25 members of the U.S. [National Academy of Sciences](/wiki/National_Academy_of_Sciences "National Academy of Sciences"),[[12]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEChiang200820–25-13) in addition to [Nobel Prize](/wiki/Nobel_Prize "Nobel Prize"),[[b]](#cite_note-14) [Turing Award](/wiki/Turing_Award "Turing Award"),[[c]](#cite_note-15) and [Wolf Prize](/wiki/Wolf_Prize "Wolf Prize") laureates.[[d]](#cite_note-16) It has also produced the heads of other major universities, such as the [University of California, Berkeley](/wiki/University_of_California,_Berkeley "University of California, Berkeley"),[[e]](#cite_note-17) and the [University of California, Santa Barbara](/wiki/University_of_California,_Santa_Barbara "University of California, Santa Barbara").[[f]](#cite_note-18)

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=National_Taiwan_University&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

### Imperial University (1928–1945)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=National_Taiwan_University&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Imperial University (1928–1945)")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/ae/Taihoku_Imperial_University.JPG/250px-Taihoku_Imperial_University.JPG)](/wiki/File:Taihoku_Imperial_University.JPG)

View of the entrance of Taihoku Imperial University, pictured during [Japanese colonial rule](/wiki/Japanese_rule_of_Taiwan "Japanese rule of Taiwan") (1895–1945)

During the [Japanese rule of Taiwan](/wiki/Taiwan_under_Japanese_rule "Taiwan under Japanese rule") (1895–1945), the [Empire of Japan](/wiki/Empire_of_Japan "Empire of Japan") established the modern Taiwanese education system by installing educational institutions that used Western-style academic systems.[[13]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELo201419–20-19) [Den Kenjirō](/wiki/Den_Kenjir%C5%8D "Den Kenjirō"), the [Governor-General of Taiwan](/wiki/Governor-General_of_Taiwan "Governor-General of Taiwan"), proposed the establishment of a university in Taiwan in 1922 and Japanese prime minister [Tanaka Giichi](/wiki/Tanaka_Giichi "Tanaka Giichi") presented a bill titled "Establishment of the Taiwan Imperial University" to the [Cabinet of Japan](/wiki/Cabinet_of_Japan "Cabinet of Japan") on February 25, 1928.[[14]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEChiang200812–13-20) It was planned to be located on the grounds of the Taihoku Senior School of Agriculture and Forestry in [Taihoku Prefecture](/wiki/Taihoku_Prefecture "Taihoku Prefecture").[[15]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEChiang200813-21)

On March 16, 1928, National Taiwan University was founded as "Taihoku Imperial University" ([Japanese](/wiki/Japanese_language "Japanese language"): 台北帝国大学, [romanized](/wiki/Romanization_of_Japanese "Romanization of Japanese"): *Taihoku Teikoku Daigaku*; [Chinese](/wiki/Traditional_Chinese_characters "Traditional Chinese characters"): 臺北帝國大學; [pinyin](/wiki/Pinyin "Pinyin"): *Táiběi dìguó dàxué*), the seventh of the Japanese Empire's [Imperial Universities](/wiki/Imperial_Universities "Imperial Universities").[[16]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGraceHouChiangChan20206-22)[[17]](#cite_note-:0-23) It was Taiwan's first and only university and primarily served to promote [Japanese culture](/wiki/Japanese_culture "Japanese culture"), assimilate the local population, and direct students to professions useful to colonial expansion.[[18]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELo201420-24) The first [freshman](/wiki/Freshman "Freshman") class was inaugurated on April 30, 1928, with classes beginning on May 5. Of the 1931 graduating class, 41 students were Japanese and five were Taiwanese.[[15]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEChiang200813-21)[[g]](#cite_note-25)

The first faculties founded at Taihoku Imperial University were the Faculty of Literature and Politics and the Faculty of Science and Agriculture, totalling 59 students. Subsequently, the Faculty of Medicine was established in 1935 and the Faculty of Engineering was established in 1943.[[16]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGraceHouChiangChan20206-22) The Faculty of Science and Agriculture was divided in 1943 as two separate colleges: the Faculty of Science and the Faculty of Agriculture.[[19]](#cite_note-:1-26) Because the university was considered a part of expanding the [Japanese colonial empire](/wiki/Japanese_colonial_empire "Japanese colonial empire") in the [Pacific Ocean](/wiki/Pacific_Ocean "Pacific Ocean"), it was supported by multiple Japanese scholars and received government [research grants](/wiki/Research_grant "Research grant") for funding policy programs.[[16]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGraceHouChiangChan20206-22) Taiwanese students could not compete with Japanese students since the university prioritized Japanese enrollment.[[16]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGraceHouChiangChan20206-22) From 1928 to 1943, the university's student body was approximately 80 percent Japanese and 20 percent Taiwanese.[[20]](#cite_note-27) Of its more than 300 faculty members in 1940, all professorships but one were held by Japanese scholars.[[21]](#cite_note-28)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/4e/Cca110001-hp-pb1203016p024-i.jpg/250px-Cca110001-hp-pb1203016p024-i.jpg)](/wiki/File:Cca110001-hp-pb1203016p024-i.jpg)

Aerial view of the university during the 1930s

Taihoku college classes consisted of "lectures" taught by professors, assistant professors, and other faculty. By 1945, it had five colleges with a total of 114 lectures.[[19]](#cite_note-:1-26) The university's first president was Japanese historian [Taira Shidehara](/w/index.php?title=Taira_Shidehara&action=edit&redlink=1 "Taira Shidehara (page does not exist)") [[ja](https://ja.wikipedia.org/wiki/%E5%B9%A3%E5%8E%9F%E5%9D%A6 "ja:幣原坦")] (1928–1937), a graduate of [Tokyo Imperial University](/wiki/Tokyo_Imperial_University "Tokyo Imperial University") who was appointed to the presidency on March 16, 1928.[[22]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEChiang200812,_13-29) Japanese scholar [Toyohachi Fujita](/wiki/Toyohachi_Fujita "Toyohachi Fujita") (1869–1929) was appointed as the first dean of the Faculty of Literature and Politics while Kintaro Oshima was named the inaugural dean of the Faculty of Science and Agriculture.[[15]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEChiang200813-21) Enrollment years were shortened during [World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II") and university functions were limited following the American [bombing of Taipei](/wiki/Raid_on_Taipei "Raid on Taipei"),[[23]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEChiang200814–15-30) which damaged its main gate and boulevard.[[24]](#cite_note-31)

### National University (1945–present)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=National_Taiwan_University&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: National University (1945–present)")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/86/%E8%A1%8C%E6%94%BF%E9%99%A2%E9%95%B7%E8%94%A3%E7%B6%93%E5%9C%8B%E5%B7%A1%E8%A6%96%E5%A4%A7%E5%AD%B8%E8%81%AF%E8%80%83%E8%87%BA%E5%A4%A7%E8%80%83%E5%A0%B4_03.jpg/250px-%E8%A1%8C%E6%94%BF%E9%99%A2%E9%95%B7%E8%94%A3%E7%B6%93%E5%9C%8B%E5%B7%A1%E8%A6%96%E5%A4%A7%E5%AD%B8%E8%81%AF%E8%80%83%E8%87%BA%E5%A4%A7%E8%80%83%E5%A0%B4_03.jpg)](/wiki/File:%E8%A1%8C%E6%94%BF%E9%99%A2%E9%95%B7%E8%94%A3%E7%B6%93%E5%9C%8B%E5%B7%A1%E8%A6%96%E5%A4%A7%E5%AD%B8%E8%81%AF%E8%80%83%E8%87%BA%E5%A4%A7%E8%80%83%E5%A0%B4_03.jpg)

Taiwanese president [Chiang Ching-kuo](/wiki/Chiang_Ching-kuo "Chiang Ching-kuo") visiting NTU in 1977

After the [Surrender of Japan](/wiki/Surrender_of_Japan "Surrender of Japan") in September 1945, the government of the [Republic of China](/wiki/Republic_of_China_(1912%E2%80%931949) "Republic of China (1912–1949)") (ROC) assumed control of the university and initiated [sinicization](/wiki/Sinicization "Sinicization") reforms. On August 15, 1945, the [Kuomintang](/wiki/Kuomintang "Kuomintang") government appointed [Lo Tsung-lo](/wiki/Lo_Tsung-lo "Lo Tsung-lo"), a Japanese-educated academic, to oversee the transition of Taihoku's curriculum, teaching system, and faculties from its Japanese administration. At the time, the university had 1,614 faculty and staff members to teach 1,767 students, 351 of whom were Taiwanese. All Japanese students were later transferred back to Japan.[[25]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEChiang200815-32)

Under the Kuomintang, the ROC government initiated a program of reforming all universities and colleges in accordance with Chinese models that incorporated American academics, administration, and organization, in addition to installing American curriculum and degree requirements.[[26]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGraceHouChiangChan20206–7-33) Reforms also had the goal of reversing the [Japanization](/wiki/Japanization "Japanization") that had influenced Taiwan during Japanese rule.[[18]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELo201420-24) Universities and colleges were opened to Taiwanese students without restrictions; Taihoku Imperial University was renamed "National Taiwan University" in November 1945 and it was reorganized and expanded to six faculties: Liberal Arts, Law, Science, Medicine, Engineering, and Agriculture.[[27]](#cite_note-34) Up to 500 students could enroll in each faculty and the enrollment period was standardized to four years as opposed to the Japanese system of three to six years.[[28]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGraceHouChiangChan20207-35) Under governor [Chen Yi](/wiki/Chen_Yi_(Kuomintang) "Chen Yi (Kuomintang)"), however, the school received no government funding from 1945 to 1947, causing it to cease operations until Chinese historian [Fu Ssu-nien](/wiki/Fu_Ssu-nien "Fu Ssu-nien") assumed the presidency.[[29]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEChouChingHawkinsJacob201225-36) Fu oversaw the abolition of the [Japanese college-preparatory school system](/wiki/Higher_school_(Japan) "Higher school (Japan)") and, by the end of 1947, the university operated entirely on a Chinese higher education model.[[29]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEChouChingHawkinsJacob201225-36) The university became the most prominent educational institution in the country, with more than 80 percent of all Taiwanese high school applicants listing the university as their preferred choice for college admission by 1977.[[30]](#cite_note-37)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/46/%E8%87%BA%E7%81%A3%E5%A4%A7%E5%AD%B8%E5%8E%9F%E5%B8%9D%E5%A4%A7%E6%A0%A1%E8%88%8D%E2%94%80%E8%88%8A%E7%B8%BD%E5%9C%96%E6%9B%B8%E9%A4%A8%28%E7%8F%BE%E6%A0%A1%E5%8F%B2%E9%A4%A8%29.JPG/250px-%E8%87%BA%E7%81%A3%E5%A4%A7%E5%AD%B8%E5%8E%9F%E5%B8%9D%E5%A4%A7%E6%A0%A1%E8%88%8D%E2%94%80%E8%88%8A%E7%B8%BD%E5%9C%96%E6%9B%B8%E9%A4%A8%28%E7%8F%BE%E6%A0%A1%E5%8F%B2%E9%A4%A8%29.JPG)](/wiki/File:%E8%87%BA%E7%81%A3%E5%A4%A7%E5%AD%B8%E5%8E%9F%E5%B8%9D%E5%A4%A7%E6%A0%A1%E8%88%8D%E2%94%80%E8%88%8A%E7%B8%BD%E5%9C%96%E6%9B%B8%E9%A4%A8(%E7%8F%BE%E6%A0%A1%E5%8F%B2%E9%A4%A8).JPG)

The Old Main Library building (pictured) was repurposed as a gallery dedicated to NTU's history.[[31]](#cite_note-38)

In the following decades after World War II, National Taiwan University underwent rapid expansion.[[19]](#cite_note-:1-26) A [night school](/wiki/Night_school "Night school") was established to provide [continuing education](/wiki/Continuing_education "Continuing education") for adults in 1955 and the NTU Research Library was completed in 1968.[[12]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEChiang200820–25-13) The College of Management, the College of Public Health, and the College of Electrical Engineering were established in 1987, 1993, and 1997, respectively. The NTU Department of Law was expanded to the NTU College of Law in 1999 and the College of Life Science was established in 2003.[[19]](#cite_note-:1-26) In November 2003, the university consisted of ten colleges, 52 academic departments, 82 graduate institutes, 1,778 full-time faculty, and more than 27,000 students.[[32]](#cite_note-39) By 2009, NTU grew to 54 departments, 100 graduate institutes (which offered 100 master's programs and 91 doctoral programs in total), and 25 research centers, including the Center for Condensed Matter Sciences, the Center for Biotechnology, the Japanese Research Center, and others.[[33]](#cite_note-40)

Koo Chen-Fu Memorial Library, designed by Japanese architect [Toyo Ito](/wiki/Toyo_Ito "Toyo Ito")

The main campus of National Taiwan University consists of 114.72 hectares (283.5 acres) in the [Gongguan area](/wiki/Gongguan,_Taipei "Gongguan, Taipei") of [Daan District](/wiki/Daan_District,_Taipei_City "Daan District, Taipei City"), [Taipei City](/wiki/Taipei "Taipei").[[34]](#cite_note-:3-41) It contains most of the university's departmental and administrative buildings.[[34]](#cite_note-:3-41)

The main campus houses some of the country's best-preserved examples of [red brick](/wiki/Red_brick "Red brick") Japanese colonial architecture, most of which are located along the Royal Palm Boulevard that leads to the five-story [National Taiwan University Library](/wiki/National_Taiwan_University_Library "National Taiwan University Library") building (completed in 1998),[[35]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECheung20258-42) in addition to the Old Main Library, the College of Liberal Arts, and the Administration Building.[[36]](#cite_note-43) Of the campus buildings built after World War II, the majority are a hybrid of Chinese and Western architecture, 20 of which were designed by architect [Wang Da-hong](/wiki/Wang_Da-hong "Wang Da-hong").[[37]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECheung20259-44) The Royal Palm Boulevard, which anchors the university's central avenue, is designed in a [Baroque](/wiki/Baroque_architecture "Baroque architecture") architectural style.[[38]](#cite_note-:6-45): 67 The boulevard forms three plazas along its axis—University Plaza, Fu Bell Plaza, and the Main Library plaza—which altogether function as student venues.[[38]](#cite_note-:6-45): 67

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/6a/NTU_main_gate_20201212.jpg/250px-NTU_main_gate_20201212.jpg)](/wiki/File:NTU_main_gate_20201212.jpg)

The Main Gate marks the university's entrance

Several historic landmarks and green spaces are located throughout the campus. The first is the red-brick Main Gate, completed in 1931, which marks the campus entrance. The gate retains much of its original appearance from the Taihoku Imperial University era and is designated a municipal heritage site. It leads to the University Promenade, an open plaza used for gatherings and public expression.[[39]](#cite_note-:5-46) Nearby is the College of Liberal Arts, constructed during Japanese rule in [Romanesque fashion](/wiki/Romanesque_architecture "Romanesque architecture"), houses the university's landmark Fu Bell which rings 21 times at the end of each school period.[[37]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECheung20259-44) Another landmark is Fu Garden, originally established as an arboretum during the Japanese colonial period, which contains a monument and mausoleum commemorating former university president [Fu Ssu-nien](/wiki/Fu_Ssu-nien "Fu Ssu-nien"). Adjacent to the garden is the Old Main Library (1929), which today houses the Gallery of NTU History, a museum documenting the university's development.[[39]](#cite_note-:5-46)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/eb/NTU_Humanities_Building_20241117_01.jpg/250px-NTU_Humanities_Building_20241117_01.jpg)](/wiki/File:NTU_Humanities_Building_20241117_01.jpg)

The Humanities Building, a complex of the College of Liberal Arts

The campus also includes several natural and landscaped features. Drunken Moon Lake, originally used to regulate water flow from the Liugongjun irrigation canal, was redesigned in the 1970s with an arched bridge and pavilion and later renovated as an ecological lakeshore area. The Agricultural Experimental Farm, established prior to the founding of Taihoku Imperial University, remains an active research and teaching facility operated by the College of Bioresources and Agriculture.[[39]](#cite_note-:5-46)

Other notable campus landmarks include the First Student Activity Center, designed by architect [Wang Da-hong](/wiki/Wang_Da-hong "Wang Da-hong") in a mixed traditional Chinese and modernist style and currently serving as a hub for student clubs and events;[[40]](#cite_note-47) the Agricultural Exhibition Hall (1963), originally built to display Taiwan's agricultural achievements; and the modern College of Social Sciences building designed by Japanese architect [Toyo Ito](/wiki/Toyo_Ito "Toyo Ito").[[39]](#cite_note-:5-46)

[National Taiwan University Hospital](/wiki/National_Taiwan_University_Hospital "National Taiwan University Hospital")

### Satellite campuses

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=National_Taiwan_University&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Satellite campuses")]

The university has four additional campuses throughout Taiwan: the Shuiyuan Campus (7.7 hectares, located in [Zhongzheng District](/wiki/Zhongzheng_District "Zhongzheng District"), [Taipei](/wiki/Taipei "Taipei")); the College of Medicine Campus (located in [Zhongzheng District](/wiki/Zhongzheng_District "Zhongzheng District")); the Yunlin Campus (54 hectares, located in [Yunlin County](/wiki/Yunlin_County "Yunlin County")); and the Zhubei Campus (22 hectares, located in [Hsinchu County](/wiki/Hsinchu_County "Hsinchu County")). The university also governs farms, forests, and hospitals for education and research purposes, including: visiting professor residences (34 hectares, located on [Yangmingshan](/wiki/Yangmingshan "Yangmingshan"), [Taipei](/wiki/Taipei "Taipei")); the NTU University Farm (19.5 hectares, located in [Xindian District](/wiki/Xindian_District "Xindian District"), [New Taipei City](/wiki/New_Taipei_City "New Taipei City")); the Wenshan Botanical Garden (5 hectares, located in [Shiding District](/wiki/Shiding_District "Shiding District"), [New Taipei City](/wiki/New_Taipei_City "New Taipei City")); the Experimental Forest Office (25.9 hectares, located in [Nantou County](/wiki/Nantou_County "Nantou County")); and the Experimental Forest (33,310 hectares, located in [Nantou County](/wiki/Nantou_County "Nantou County")).[[34]](#cite_note-:3-41) The total area of all NTU campuses and properties is 34,166 hectares, or 1 percent of all of the land area in Taiwan.[[17]](#cite_note-:0-23)[[41]](#cite_note-48): 7

## Organization and academics

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=National_Taiwan_University&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Organization and academics")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/2f/National_Taiwan_University_Library_20201117.jpg/250px-National_Taiwan_University_Library_20201117.jpg)](/wiki/File:National_Taiwan_University_Library_20201117.jpg)

[National Taiwan University Library](/wiki/National_Taiwan_University_Library "National Taiwan University Library")

As of 2025, National Taiwan University consists of seventeen colleges, including Liberal Arts, Engineering, Science, Social Sciences, Political Science & Economics, Law, Bio-Resources & Agriculture, Management, Public Health, Electrical Engineering & Computer Science, Medicine, Life Science, General Education, the International College, the Graduate School of Advanced Technology, Design & Innovation, and Continuing Studies.[[9]](#cite_note-:2-10) They offer [bachelor's degrees](/wiki/Bachelor%27s_degrees "Bachelor's degrees"), [master's degrees](/wiki/Master%27s_degrees "Master's degrees"), and [doctoral degrees](/wiki/Doctoral_degrees "Doctoral degrees") in multiple disciplines and specializations across science, arts, and the humanities. Some majors are considered more competitive than others and require a higher score in the [General Scholastic Ability Test](/wiki/General_Scholastic_Ability_Test "General Scholastic Ability Test") or other national examinations. In recent years, [medicine](/wiki/Medicine "Medicine"), [electrical engineering](/wiki/Electrical_engineering "Electrical engineering"), [law](/wiki/Law "Law"), and [finance](/wiki/Finance "Finance") have been the most selective majors. Most majors take four years to complete while the dental program and the medical program take six years to finish.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/70/%E7%9B%B4%E8%BD%84%E5%B8%82%E5%AE%9A%E5%8F%A4%E8%B9%9F%E8%87%BA%E5%A4%A7%E9%86%AB%E9%99%A2%E8%88%8A%E9%A4%A8.jpg/250px-%E7%9B%B4%E8%BD%84%E5%B8%82%E5%AE%9A%E5%8F%A4%E8%B9%9F%E8%87%BA%E5%A4%A7%E9%86%AB%E9%99%A2%E8%88%8A%E9%A4%A8.jpg)](/wiki/File:%E7%9B%B4%E8%BD%84%E5%B8%82%E5%AE%9A%E5%8F%A4%E8%B9%9F%E8%87%BA%E5%A4%A7%E9%86%AB%E9%99%A2%E8%88%8A%E9%A4%A8.jpg)

The original NTU hospital building, built in 1895, was the largest in [Southeast Asia](/wiki/Southeast_Asia "Southeast Asia").[[42]](#cite_note-49)

Students are able to select courses offered by any of the colleges, with up to 8,000 courses made available for selection each semester. Undergraduate students are required to take a mandatory [core curriculum](/wiki/Core_curriculum "Core curriculum"), which is composed of courses in Chinese, English, physical education, and public service. The medical school also requires each of its students to take [philosophy](/wiki/Philosophy "Philosophy") and [sociology](/wiki/Sociology "Sociology") classes as well as seminars in [ethics](/wiki/Ethics "Ethics") and [thanatology](/wiki/Thanatology "Thanatology"). [Military training](/wiki/Conscription_in_Taiwan "Conscription in Taiwan") is no longer an obligatory course for male students, but it is a prerequisite if they plan to apply to become officers during their compulsory military service. Most students at the university come from wealthy families with higher [socioeconomic status](/wiki/Socioeconomic_status "Socioeconomic status").[[43]](#cite_note-50)

NTU is a member of the [Association of Pacific Rim Universities](/wiki/Association_of_Pacific_Rim_Universities "Association of Pacific Rim Universities"),[[44]](#cite_note-51) the [Association of East Asian Research Universities](/wiki/Association_of_East_Asian_Research_Universities "Association of East Asian Research Universities"), the [Association of Southeast Asian Institutions of Higher Learning](/wiki/Association_of_Southeast_Asian_Institutions_of_Higher_Learning "Association of Southeast Asian Institutions of Higher Learning"), the [Association to Advance Collegiate Schools of Business](/wiki/Association_to_Advance_Collegiate_Schools_of_Business "Association to Advance Collegiate Schools of Business"), and the [European Union Centre in Taiwan](/wiki/European_Union_Centre_in_Taiwan "European Union Centre in Taiwan"). It also has partnerships with several American universities such as [Washington University in St. Louis](/wiki/Washington_University_in_St._Louis "Washington University in St. Louis"), which, since 2012, has funded NTU students to complete doctoral degrees at the university.[[45]](#cite_note-52) In 2024, NTU and the [University of Texas at Arlington](/wiki/University_of_Texas_at_Arlington "University of Texas at Arlington") began a partnership to host a dual master's degree program in architecture between the two universities.[[46]](#cite_note-53)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/88/NTU_Astronomy_Mathematics_Building_south_side_20130905.jpg/250px-NTU_Astronomy_Mathematics_Building_south_side_20130905.jpg)](/wiki/File:NTU_Astronomy_Mathematics_Building_south_side_20130905.jpg)

Astronomy and Mathematics Building

The [International Chinese Language Program](/wiki/International_Chinese_Language_Program "International Chinese Language Program") (ICLP), founded by [Stanford University](/wiki/Stanford_University "Stanford University"), is located at National Taiwan University.[[47]](#cite_note-54)

In 2021, the "International College" was established, primarily enrolling international students of foreign nationality and offering courses entirely in English.[[48]](#cite_note-55) In July 2025, the [Max Planck Society](/wiki/Max_Planck_Society "Max Planck Society") and the [Institute for Advanced Study](/wiki/Institute_for_Advanced_Study "Institute for Advanced Study") established the Max-Planck-IAS-NTU Center, a [research center](/wiki/Research_center "Research center") dedicated to studying [particle physics](/wiki/Particle_physics "Particle physics"), [cosmology](/wiki/Cosmology "Cosmology"), and [geometry](/wiki/Geometry "Geometry") on campus.[[49]](#cite_note-56)

### Overall rankings

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=National_Taiwan_University&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Overall rankings")]

National Taiwan University is widely considered to be the best university in Taiwan. Most high school applicants generally consider NTU to be their most preferred choice for college.[[60]](#cite_note-:4-67)

NTU was ranked 63rd worldwide in the [QS World University Rankings](/wiki/QS_World_University_Rankings "QS World University Rankings") 2026,[[61]](#cite_note-68) 140th worldwide in the [Times Higher Education World University Rankings](/wiki/Times_Higher_Education_World_University_Rankings "Times Higher Education World University Rankings") 2026, 203rd worldwide in the US News 2022-2023, and 201-300th worldwide in the ARWU 2022.

The Aggregate Ranking of Top Universities (ARTU), which sorts universities based on their aggregate performance across THE, QS, and ARWU, ranked NTU 135th worldwide in 2022.[[62]](#cite_note-69)

With other peering references of academic ranking, NTU also releases NTU World Universities ranking annually on the [Double Ten Day](/wiki/National_Day_of_the_Republic_of_China "National Day of the Republic of China"), the National Holiday of the [Republic of China](/wiki/Republic_of_China "Republic of China").[[63]](#cite_note-70)

### Subject rankings

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=National_Taiwan_University&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Subject rankings")]

In the QS and ARWU subject rankings, NTU is ranked first in Taiwan in the majority of subjects.[[64]](#cite_note-QSSubject-71)[[65]](#cite_note-ARWUSubject-72) In the THE Subject Rankings, NTU is ranked first in Taiwan in all subjects.[[66]](#cite_note-THESubject-73)

Chee-Chun Leung Hall, completed in 2017, was designed by architect [Kris Yao](/wiki/Kris_Yao "Kris Yao") and houses one of the university's [particle astrophysics](/wiki/Particle_astrophysics "Particle astrophysics") research centers.[[67]](#cite_note-74)

The president heads the university. Each college is headed by a dean and each department by a chairman. Students elect their own representatives each year to attend administrative meetings.

National Taiwan University has produced notable alumni in politics, business, academia, science, medicine, and numerous other fields. Graduates of the university disproportionately comprise a majority of the political and business elite in Taiwan.[[60]](#cite_note-:4-67) As of 2024, about half (48.7%) of all [academicians](/wiki/Academician "Academician") of [Academia Sinica](/wiki/Academia_Sinica "Academia Sinica") are NTU graduates and 70 percent of all Taiwanese members of the U.S. [National Academy of Sciences](/wiki/National_Academy_of_Sciences "National Academy of Sciences") (NAS) are.[[68]](#cite_note-75) 25 graduates of NTU are members of the U.S. National Academy of Sciences.[[69]](#cite_note-76)

Five of the eight [presidents of the Republic of China](/wiki/Presidents_of_the_Republic_of_China "Presidents of the Republic of China") are graduates of the university: [Lai Ching-te](/wiki/Lai_Ching-Te "Lai Ching-Te"), the current president of Taiwan; [Tsai Ing-Wen](/wiki/Tsai_Ing-Wen "Tsai Ing-Wen"), the first woman to be elected to the position; [Ma Ying-jeou](/wiki/Ma_Ying-jeou "Ma Ying-jeou"); [Chen Shui-bian](/wiki/Chen_Shui-bian "Chen Shui-bian"), the first member of the [Democratic Progressive Party](/wiki/Democratic_Progressive_Party "Democratic Progressive Party") (DPP) to hold the office; and [Lee Teng-hui](/wiki/Lee_Teng-hui "Lee Teng-hui"), the first native-born Taiwanese to become president. In addition, six out of the 13 [vice-presidents of the Republic of China](/wiki/Vice_President_of_the_Republic_of_China "Vice President of the Republic of China") have graduated from NTU, including Lee Teng-hui, [Lien Chan](/wiki/Lien_Chan "Lien Chan"), [Annette Lu](/wiki/Annette_Lu "Annette Lu"), [Wu Den-yih](/wiki/Wu_Den-yih "Wu Den-yih"), [Chen Chien-jen](/wiki/Chen_Chien-jen "Chen Chien-jen"), and Lai Ching-te. The heads of major political parties—such as [Eric Chu](/wiki/Eric_Chu "Eric Chu") and [Cheng Li-wun](/wiki/Cheng_Li-wun "Cheng Li-wun"), [chairs of the Kuomintang](/wiki/Chairman_of_the_Kuomintang "Chairman of the Kuomintang"); [Ko Wen-je](/wiki/Ko_Wen-je "Ko Wen-je"), former mayor of Taipei and founder of the [Taiwan People's Party](/wiki/Taiwan_People%27s_Party "Taiwan People's Party") (TPP); and [Huang Kuo-chang](/wiki/Huang_Kuo-chang "Huang Kuo-chang"), chairman of the TPP—also graduated from NTU.

In science, graduates include [Yuan T. Lee](/wiki/Yuan_T._Lee "Yuan T. Lee"), who received the 1986 [Nobel Prize in Chemistry](/wiki/Nobel_Prize_in_Chemistry "Nobel Prize in Chemistry") for the development of [reaction dynamics](/wiki/Reaction_dynamics "Reaction dynamics"), and [Andrew Yao](/wiki/Andrew_Yao "Andrew Yao"), who was awarded the [Turing Award](/wiki/Turing_Award "Turing Award") in 2000 for his contributions to [cryptography](/wiki/Cryptography "Cryptography") and [computation](/wiki/Computation "Computation"). Other scientific achievements by alumni include contributions to [chemical synthesis](/wiki/Chemical_synthesis "Chemical synthesis") by chemist [Chi-Huey Wong](/wiki/Chi-Huey_Wong "Chi-Huey Wong"), winner of the 2014 [Wolf Prize in Chemistry](/wiki/Wolf_Prize_in_Chemistry "Wolf Prize in Chemistry"), and contributions to [biosynthesis](/wiki/Biosynthesis "Biosynthesis") by botanist [Shang Fa Yang](/wiki/Shang_Fa_Yang "Shang Fa Yang"), winner of the 1991 [Wolf Prize in Agriculture](/wiki/Wolf_Prize_in_Agriculture "Wolf Prize in Agriculture"). Alumnus [Chenming Hu](/wiki/Chenming_Hu "Chenming Hu") was awarded the [IEEE Medal of Honor](/wiki/IEEE_Medal_of_Honor "IEEE Medal of Honor") in 2020; [Simon Sze](/wiki/Simon_Sze "Simon Sze") the [J. J. Ebers Award](/wiki/J._J._Ebers_Award "J. J. Ebers Award") in 1991; and [George Kuo](/wiki/George_Kuo "George Kuo") the 1994 [William Beaumont Prize](/wiki/William_Beaumont_Prize "William Beaumont Prize") for the discovery of the [Hepatitis C virus](/wiki/Hepatitis_C_virus "Hepatitis C virus"). Notable mathematicians who graduated from NTU include [Fan Chung](/wiki/Fan_Chung "Fan Chung"), the Paul Erdős Professor in Combinatorics at the [University of California, San Diego](/wiki/University_of_California,_San_Diego "University of California, San Diego"); [Wu-Chung Hsiang](/wiki/Wu-Chung_Hsiang "Wu-Chung Hsiang"), topologist and professor at [Princeton University](/wiki/Princeton_University "Princeton University"); and [Horng-Tzer Yau](/wiki/Horng-Tzer_Yau "Horng-Tzer Yau"), Merton Professor of Mathematics at [Harvard University](/wiki/Harvard_University "Harvard University").

In academia, alumni include two chancellors of American universities: [Chang-Lin Tien](/wiki/Chang-Lin_Tien "Chang-Lin Tien"), chancellor of the [University of California, Berkeley](/wiki/University_of_California,_Berkeley "University of California, Berkeley"), and [Henry T. Yang](/wiki/Henry_T._Yang "Henry T. Yang"), chancellor of the [University of California, Santa Barbara](/wiki/University_of_California,_Santa_Barbara "University of California, Santa Barbara"). Other [academic administrators](/wiki/Academic_administration "Academic administration") include [Ambrose King](/wiki/Ambrose_King "Ambrose King"), vice-chancellor of the [Chinese University of Hong Kong](/wiki/Chinese_University_of_Hong_Kong "Chinese University of Hong Kong"), and [Vincent Chang](/wiki/Vincent_Chang_(academic) "Vincent Chang (academic)"), vice-chancellor of [BRAC University](/wiki/BRAC_University "BRAC University"). Academicians of Academia Sinica include political scientist [Chu Yun-han](/wiki/Chu_Yun-han "Chu Yun-han"); economists [Hu Sheng-cheng](/wiki/Hu_Sheng-cheng "Hu Sheng-cheng"), [Cyrus Chu](/wiki/Cyrus_Chu "Cyrus Chu"), [Cheng Hsiao](/wiki/Cheng_Hsiao "Cheng Hsiao"), [Yu Tzong-shian](/wiki/Yu_Tzong-shian "Yu Tzong-shian"), [Mai Chao-cheng](/wiki/Mai_Chao-cheng "Mai Chao-cheng"), and [George C. Tiao](/wiki/George_C._Tiao "George C. Tiao"); and legal scholars [Hu Fo](/wiki/Hu_Fo "Hu Fo") and [Hungdah Chiu](/wiki/Hungdah_Chiu "Hungdah Chiu").[[70]](#cite_note-77)

Many of the university's graduates have gone on to found or head major companies, including [Quanta Computer](/wiki/Quanta_Computer "Quanta Computer")'s [Barry Lam](/wiki/Barry_Lam "Barry Lam"), [Mediatek](/wiki/Mediatek "Mediatek")'s [Tsai Ming-kai](/wiki/Tsai_Ming-kai "Tsai Ming-kai") and [Garmin](/wiki/Garmin "Garmin")'s [Min Kao](/wiki/Min_Kao "Min Kao").

Foreign alumni of the university include Canadian philosopher [Roger T. Ames](/wiki/Roger_T._Ames "Roger T. Ames"), who graduated from NTU with a master's degree in philosophy in 1972; British diplomat [David Coates](/wiki/David_Coates_(diplomat) "David Coates (diplomat)"), [ambassador of the United Kingdom to Ivory Coast](/wiki/List_of_ambassadors_of_the_United_Kingdom_to_Ivory_Coast "List of ambassadors of the United Kingdom to Ivory Coast"); [Roel Sterckx](/wiki/Roel_Sterckx "Roel Sterckx"), Joseph Needham Professor of Chinese History at the [University of Cambridge](/wiki/University_of_Cambridge "University of Cambridge"); and South Korean philosopher [Do-ol](/wiki/Do-ol "Do-ol"), who earned a master's degree from NTU.

2. **[^](#cite_ref-14)** Chemist [Yuan T. Lee](/wiki/Yuan_T._Lee "Yuan T. Lee"), a 1959 graduate of National Taiwan University, won the 1986 [Nobel Prize in Chemistry](/wiki/Nobel_Prize_in_Chemistry "Nobel Prize in Chemistry") with [John C. Polanyi](/wiki/John_C._Polanyi "John C. Polanyi") and [Dudley R. Herschbach](/wiki/Dudley_R._Herschbach "Dudley R. Herschbach").
3. **[^](#cite_ref-15)** After graduating from NTU in 1967, [Andrew Yao](/wiki/Andrew_Yao "Andrew Yao") earned a [Ph.D.](/wiki/Ph.D. "Ph.D.") in physics from [Harvard University](/wiki/Harvard_University "Harvard University") and a second doctorate from the [University of Illinois at Urbana-Champaign](/wiki/University_of_Illinois_at_Urbana-Champaign "University of Illinois at Urbana-Champaign"). He was awarded the [Turing Award](/wiki/Turing_Award "Turing Award") in 2000 for contributions to [computer science](/wiki/Computer_science "Computer science").
4. **[^](#cite_ref-16)** Botanist [Shang Fa Yang](/wiki/Shang_Fa_Yang "Shang Fa Yang") graduated with his bachelor's degree and his master's degree from NTU In 1956 and 1958, respectively, and was awarded the 1991 [Wolf Prize in Agriculture](/wiki/Wolf_Prize_in_Agriculture "Wolf Prize in Agriculture"). Biochemist [Chi-Huey Wong](/wiki/Chi-Huey_Wong "Chi-Huey Wong") graduated from NTU with his bachelor's degree and master's degree in 1970 and 1977, respectively, and received the 2014 [Wolf Prize in Chemistry](/wiki/Wolf_Prize_in_Chemistry "Wolf Prize in Chemistry").
5. **[^](#cite_ref-17)** Alumnus [Chang-Lin Tien](/wiki/Chang-Lin_Tien "Chang-Lin Tien"), who earned his [Bachelor of Science](/wiki/Bachelor_of_Science "Bachelor of Science") in [mechanical engineering](/wiki/Mechanical_engineering "Mechanical engineering") from NTU in 1955, served as the [chancellor](/wiki/Chancellor_(education) "Chancellor (education)") of the [University of California, Berkeley](/wiki/University_of_California,_Berkeley "University of California, Berkeley"), from 1990 to 1997.
6. **[^](#cite_ref-18)** Alumnus [Henry T. Yang](/wiki/Henry_T._Yang "Henry T. Yang"), who earned his [Bachelor of Science](/wiki/Bachelor_of_Science "Bachelor of Science") in [civil engineering](/wiki/Civil_engineering "Civil engineering") from NTU in 1962, served as the chancellor of the [University of California, Santa Barbara](/wiki/University_of_California,_Santa_Barbara "University of California, Santa Barbara"), from 1994 to 2025.
7. **[^](#cite_ref-25)** The five Taiwanese graduates were: Seth Mackay Ko (History), De-Jyun Jhong (Politics), Ching-chung Hsu (Agriculture), Sing-wun Liu (Agriculture), and Yu-ze Cai (Agriculture).[[15]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEChiang200813-21)
